food_subgroup: Poultry — marinated and fried chicken appetiser

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for chicken lollipop masala; closest_parent 'chicken food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for chicken lollipop masala; A00LC (Chicken meat, cooked) is the nearest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 'Chicken dish (food)' is the closest available parent; no specific code exists for chicken lollipop masala.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of chicken lollipop masala consists of 4 to 5 pieces, each made from a frenched chicken wingette or drumette, weighing approximately 200 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Tandoori Chicken / Spiced Fried Chicken — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017), adapted for chicken lollipop masala preparation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for chicken lollipop masala exists in ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 2017 data for spiced/tandoori chicken (bone-in, marinated, cooked) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Chicken, drumette, cooked, fried' (FDC ID 331960). The lollipop preparation involves frenching the bone, marinating in yoghurt and spices, and deep-frying, which increases fat content relative to plain grilled chicken. Macros reflect a weighted composite estimate consistent with both sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken lollipop masala. GI is estimated at 30 based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate spiced fried chicken preparations. Protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate foods with a yoghurt-spice marinade consistently yield GI values in the 25–35 range per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 8.4) / 100 ≈ 2.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~840 mg per typical 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1–2 pieces and avoid additional salt or high-sodium dipping sauces, as a full restaurant serving can contribute approximately 840 mg sodium — around 37% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) and low carbohydrate content (~4.2 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken lollipop masala has a low glycemic index and low carbohydrate load, making it a relatively suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the deep-fried preparation adds saturated fat, so portion control and pairing with non-starchy vegetables is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.2 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (~78 mg per 100 g) from deep-frying and skin-on chicken
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-fried preparation method and use of skin-on chicken drumettes elevates saturated fat and cholesterol content; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional servings and prefer air-fried or baked variants where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Lollipop Masala is a popular Indian appetiser made from frenched chicken wingettes or drumettes — the meat is pushed down to one end of the bone to create a lollipop shape — which are then marinated in a spiced yoghurt mixture containing red chilli powder, garam masala, cumin, coriander, turmeric, ginger-garlic paste, and lemon juice, before being deep-fried until crisp and golden. The dish is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore, including at venues such as Dugout on Circular Road, and is a staple of Indo-Chinese and North Indian restaurant menus. The exterior is crisp and intensely spiced, while the interior remains juicy and tender. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-rich (~19.5 g per 100 g) and moderate in fat (~13.8 g per 100 g) due to the deep-frying process. Its glycemic index is low (~30), making it suitable for blood glucose management when consumed in controlled portions. Sodium content is notable (~420 mg per 100 g), which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is typically halal and gluten-free, though the yoghurt marinade contains dairy. It is commonly served as a starter or snack alongside mint chutney or tamarind sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken lollipop?
Is chicken lollipop masala gluten free?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for chicken lollipop masala exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN spiced/tandoori chicken data and USDA fried chicken drumette data, adjusted for the deep-fried masala preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation is methodologically sound and cross-referenced across two approved sources.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. All other micronutrient values are adapted estimates from ICMR-NIN 2017 and USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 331960). The cuisine_tag 'Butter Chicken' in the input seed record appears to be a field survey platform tagging artefact and has not been carried forward, as this dish is not butter chicken. The food_name has been normalised from 'Chicken Lolipop (Masala)' to 'Chicken Lollipop Masala' (standard English spelling). Halal status is marked true as chicken lollipop masala is conventionally prepared with halal chicken in Singapore Indian restaurants; this should be verified at the restaurant level for individual outlets. Gluten-free status is marked true as the standard recipe does not include wheat-based ingredients; cross-contamination risk in restaurant kitchens is not assessed here.
